title: CAP Theorem & Distributed Systems
description: A staff-level treatment of CAP and its consequences: what the theorem actually proves, why partition tolerance is mandatory, the consistency-model spectrum, quorum math, replication and consensus (Raft/Paxos), and how to classify and design real systems under partition and latency constraints.

blocks.text: The CAP theorem, conjectured by Eric Brewer in 2000 and formalized by Gilbert and Lynch in 2002, states that a distributed data store cannot simultaneously provide all three of: Consistency (C), Availability (A), and Partition tolerance (P). The precise and useful reading is narrower than the popular slogan: when a network partition occurs, a system must choose between remaining consistent and remaining available. Outside of partitions, the trade-off disappears and you can be both consistent and available.

items: Consistency (C) in CAP means linearizability: every read observes the most recent completed write, as if there were a single copy of the data accessed under a global clock.
Availability (A) means every request to a non-failing node returns a non-error response (eventually), even if some nodes are unreachable.
Partition tolerance (P) means the system continues to operate despite messages between nodes being arbitrarily delayed or dropped.

blocks.text: CAP is a statement about the worst case during a partition, not a steady-state classification. The truly informative question is: 'When the network splits, do you sacrifice consistency or availability?' Everything else follows from that.

Because partitions are not optional in real networks, the genuine design choice is between a CP system (refuse or block requests on the minority side to preserve a single consistent view) and an AP system (keep serving requests everywhere and reconcile divergence later). 'CA' is essentially a non-distributed system that has given up on partitions, which is why it is rarely a meaningful category for a multi-node store.

Partition tolerance is not a feature you opt into; it is a property of the physical world you must tolerate. TCP connections drop, switches fail, garbage-collection pauses freeze a node for seconds, a misconfigured firewall isolates a rack, and cross-region links suffer transient loss. From the perspective of one node, a slow peer and a dead peer are indistinguishable: you cannot tell whether a missing acknowledgement means the peer crashed, the message was lost, or the peer is simply slow. This is the FLP-flavored ambiguity at the heart of distributed systems.

If you assume partitions never happen, you have not eliminated the failure mode, you have only removed your system's ability to respond to it correctly. The partition still occurs; your code just behaves arbitrarily.

Since P must be assumed, CAP collapses to a binary choice taken during the partition window. A CP system on the minority side stops accepting writes (and possibly reads) to avoid serving stale or conflicting data. An AP system keeps accepting requests on both sides, accepting that the two sides will diverge and that conflict resolution will be required when the partition heals.

items: You can never sacrifice P in practice and remain a distributed system, so the real spectrum is CP versus AP.
The choice can be made per-operation, per-key, or per-table, not just per-system; mature databases expose tunable consistency.
Latency is the everyday cousin of this trade-off: even with no partition, stronger consistency costs round-trips. That is what PACELC captures.

blocks.text: The most damaging way CAP is taught is the Venn-diagram 'pick two of three' framing, which suggests C, A, and P are three symmetric resources and that 'CA' is a legitimate, ongoing design choice. This is wrong on two counts. First, the trade-off only manifests during a partition; when the network is healthy a well-built system is both consistent and available simultaneously. Second, P is not a property you trade away, it is an environmental condition. You do not 'choose' to drop partition tolerance any more than you choose to drop gravity.

Saying a system is 'CA' usually means 'we have not thought about partitions.' A single-node database is trivially CA only because it cannot partition; the moment you replicate it, you must answer the CP-or-AP question.

rows: C, A, P are interchangeable choices
P is mandatory; C vs A only conflicts during a partition
Networks partition regardless of your wishes
Minority side loses availability during partition
AP offers eventual/causal consistency, not zero consistency

blocks.text: A sharper mental model: think of CAP as 'if Partitioned, then Consistency-or-Availability.' The acronym would be clearer as a conditional than as a triangle. Use the conditional framing in interviews to immediately signal you understand the theorem rather than reciting the slogan.

CAP's 'C' is the strongest practical model, linearizability, but consistency is a spectrum, and most systems live somewhere between linearizable and eventual. Picking the weakest model that still satisfies your application's invariants is a core staff-engineer skill, because every step toward stronger consistency costs coordination, latency, and availability.

rows: Single global order; reads see latest committed write
All nodes see ops in same order, not necessarily real-time
Causally related ops seen in order; concurrent ops may differ
A client sees its own prior writes

blocks.text: Linearizability is a recency guarantee about single objects. Serializability is an isolation guarantee about transactions over multiple objects. Strict serializability is the combination of both. Interviewers love when you keep these orthogonal axes distinct.

Causal consistency is the strongest model that can be provided while remaining available during a partition (the COPS / 'Consistency, Availability, Convergence' line of work). That makes it the sweet spot for many AP systems: you preserve cause-and-effect ordering (a reply never appears before the message it answers) without paying for global linearizability.

sections.heading: Quorums and the W + R > N Rule

blocks.text: Leaderless, Dynamo-style systems replace a single leader with quorum reads and writes. With N replicas, a write waits for W acknowledgements and a read queries R replicas. When W + R > N, the read set and the write set are guaranteed to overlap on at least one replica, so a read will observe at least one copy of the latest acknowledged write. Tuning W and R lets you slide between consistency and availability per operation.

blocks.code:
N = 5 replicas

Strong-ish read (overlap guaranteed):
  W = 3, R = 3  ->  W + R = 6 > 5   (overlap, can tolerate 2 down)

Fast writes, risk stale reads:
  W = 1, R = 1  ->  W + R = 2 <= 5  (NO overlap, eventual only)

Write-optimized but still overlapping:
  W = 2, R = 4  ->  W + R = 6 > 5   (reads slower, writes cheap)

Tolerate F node failures for writes:  N - W >= F
  N = 5, W = 3  ->  tolerate 2 write failures

blocks.text: W + R > N gives overlap, NOT linearizability. Concurrent writes, read repair timing, sloppy quorums with hinted handoff, and non-atomic multi-replica writes can all surface stale or conflicting values. Quorums bound staleness probabilistically; they do not impose a global order.

items: W = N gives durable writes but fails if any replica is down (low availability).
R = 1, W = N favors read latency; W = 1, R = N favors write latency.
Sloppy quorums accept writes on any reachable N nodes (not the 'home' replicas), trading the overlap guarantee for higher write availability during partitions.

blocks.text: How you replicate determines where the CAP trade-off bites. The three canonical topologies are single-leader, multi-leader, and leaderless, each with distinct consistency and conflict characteristics.

rows: Read repair, vector clocks, LWW, CRDTs

blocks.text: Single-leader replication is the workhorse of relational databases. Synchronous replication to at least one follower makes failover safe but blocks writes if that follower is slow; asynchronous replication keeps writes fast but risks losing the tail of the log on failover. Semi-synchronous (ack from one of several followers) is the common compromise.

Multi-leader and leaderless systems must resolve concurrent writes. Last-write-wins (LWW) by timestamp is simple but silently drops data under clock skew. Vector clocks (or version vectors) detect concurrency precisely. Conflict-free Replicated Data Types (CRDTs) encode a merge function so that concurrent updates converge deterministically without coordination, which is why they are popular in AP systems like collaborative editors.
Choosing LWW is choosing to lose writes under concurrency. That is acceptable for a cache, dangerous for an inventory counter. Match the conflict-resolution strategy to the cost of losing an update.

Consensus algorithms let a cluster agree on a single value (or an ordered log of values) despite failures, and they are the machinery behind CP systems. A majority quorum (floor(N/2)+1) must agree before a value is committed, which is precisely why a partitioned minority cannot make progress: it cannot form a majority, so it gives up availability to preserve consistency.

Raft decomposes consensus into leader election, log replication, and safety. Each node is a Follower, Candidate, or Leader. Time is divided into terms; each term has at most one leader. A follower that hears nothing from a leader within its randomized election timeout becomes a candidate, increments the term, votes for itself, and requests votes. On receiving a majority it becomes leader and starts sending heartbeats. Randomized timeouts make split votes rare and self-correcting.

Log replication: the leader appends a client command to its log and sends AppendEntries to followers. Once a majority has persisted the entry, the leader marks it committed and applies it to the state machine; followers apply it when they learn the commit index. The election restriction (a candidate must have an up-to-date log to win) guarantees committed entries are never lost. Paxos solves the same problem and is provably correct, but its single-decree formulation and the orchestration of Multi-Paxos are notoriously hard to implement, which is exactly why Raft was designed for understandability.
Mental shortcut: consensus = majority quorum + a stable leader + a replicated log. ZooKeeper (Zab), etcd and Consul (Raft), and Spanner (Paxos) are all variations on this theme.

Split-brain is the failure where a partition (or a botched failover) leaves two nodes each believing they are the leader, so both accept writes and the data diverges. Majority quorums prevent split-brain for commits, because two disjoint majorities cannot exist in one cluster. But a stale leader may not yet know it has lost leadership and can still issue commands to a downstream resource.

Fencing tokens solve this. The lock or lease service issues a monotonically increasing token with each grant. Every write to the protected resource carries its token, and the resource rejects any token lower than the highest it has seen. A delayed write from a deposed leader carries an old (smaller) token and is refused, even though that leader still thinks it is in charge.

blocks.code:
Client A acquires lock -> token = 33
Client A pauses (long GC), lease expires
Client B acquires lock -> token = 34, writes (token 34) -> accepted
Client A wakes, writes (token 33) -> REJECTED (33 < 34)

Storage rule: accept write only if token > last_seen_token

blocks.text: A lock alone is not enough. Without fencing, a paused-then-resumed leader can corrupt state after its lease expired. Always fence at the resource, not just at the lock service.

The payoff of CAP literacy is being able to classify and choose systems. The classification is about the partition-time behavior of a given configuration, not a fixed label, because many systems are tunable. Below is how the common datastores behave in their typical configurations.

rows: Minority/followers cannot accept writes; failover needed
Serves both sides; tune QUORUM for CP-like reads
Eventual by default, strongly consistent reads available
Accepts writes on both sides, reconciles later
Single-leader replica set, majority write concern
Minority loses primary; election restores availability

blocks.text: Notice that Cassandra and DynamoDB appear AP but can be pushed toward CP per operation: a Cassandra read and write both at QUORUM give W + R > N overlap, and DynamoDB offers ConsistentRead. Conversely, an asynchronous PostgreSQL replica can serve stale reads, which is a small concession away from strict CP. Classification is therefore configuration-dependent, and that nuance is exactly what senior interviews probe.
When asked 'is X CP or AP?', answer 'in its default configuration, X is ..., but it is tunable because ...'. That phrasing demonstrates you understand the trade-off rather than memorizing a label.

CAP only describes behavior during a partition, which is rare. PACELC (Abadi, 2012) extends it to the common case: if Partitioned, choose between Availability and Consistency (PAC); Else, choose between Latency and Consistency (ELC). This captures the everyday cost: even with a perfectly healthy network, providing strong consistency requires coordination round-trips that add latency.

rows: Available under partition; low latency otherwise
Consistent under partition; pays latency for consistency

blocks.text: PACELC is the more honest model because the 'Else' branch is where your system spends 99.99% of its life. A system that is never partitioned can still be slow because it insists on linearizable reads from a quorum.

In design discussions, lead with PACELC. It shows you reason about the steady-state latency cost of consistency, not just the dramatic partition scenario, and it forces a concrete question: are this feature's reads worth a cross-region round-trip, or can they tolerate a slightly stale local replica?

The point of all this theory in an interview is to drive concrete design choices. The senior move is to partition the problem by invariant: identify which pieces of data require linearizability (money, locks, unique constraints, leader election) and isolate them in a CP store, while letting everything that tolerates staleness (feeds, counts, search indexes, recommendations) live in an AP store optimized for availability and latency.

items: Map each entity to the weakest consistency model that preserves its invariants; do not pay for linearizability you do not need.
Keep the CP core small. Put strongly-consistent state (account balances, idempotency keys) behind consensus and scale the AP edges horizontally.
Make writes idempotent so retries during partition recovery are safe.
Always fence stateful operations with monotonic tokens to survive split-brain.
Prefer causal or read-your-writes consistency at the session layer to hide eventual-consistency anomalies from users.
State your PACELC choice explicitly: under partition you favor X, and in normal operation you accept Y latency for Z consistency.

blocks.text: Strong recommendation: design CP and AP boundaries deliberately. A payment ledger behind Raft, a product catalog on an eventually-consistent replica, and a session-stickiness layer to mask staleness is a textbook senior architecture answer.

When you cannot avoid the trade-off, make it observable: emit metrics for replication lag, quorum failures, and conflict rates, and define explicit SLOs for staleness. A system whose consistency posture is measured and alerted on is far more defensible than one that merely claims to be 'strongly consistent.'

When CAP comes up, resist reciting 'pick two.' Open with the conditional framing, name P as mandatory, and pivot quickly to PACELC and the consistency spectrum to show depth. Then ground the discussion in a concrete data model and explain which parts you make CP and which AP, and why.

items: Lead with: 'During a partition you trade C for A; the rest of the time you trade latency for consistency (PACELC).'
Always classify a system by its configuration, not a fixed label.
Tie quorum math (W + R > N) to a specific N and explain what it does and does not guarantee.
Mention fencing tokens the moment locks or leader election appear.

blocks.text: A great closing line: 'Linearizability is a tool, not a default. I spend it only where an invariant demands it, and I make the staleness everywhere else explicit and bounded.'

diagrams.title: Write Succeeds on One Side of a Partition (AP)
diagrams.content:
sequenceDiagram
    participant C1 as Client West
    participant N1 as Node West
    participant N2 as Node East
    participant C2 as Client East
    Note over N1,N2: Network partition forms
    C1->>N1: write x = 5
    N1-->>N2: replicate x = 5
    Note over N1,N2: replication message dropped
    N1-->>C1: ack (AP: accept locally)
    C2->>N2: read x
    N2-->>C2: x = old (stale)
    Note over N1,N2: partition heals
    N1->>N2: anti-entropy: x = 5
    N2-->>N2: converge

diagrams.title: Raft Node State Transitions
diagrams.content:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Follower
    Follower --> Candidate: election timeout
    Candidate --> Candidate: split vote, new term
    Candidate --> Leader: wins majority
    Candidate --> Follower: sees higher term
    Leader --> Follower: sees higher term
    Follower --> Follower: receives heartbeat

diagrams.title: Quorum Read/Write Overlap (N=5, W=3, R=3)
diagrams.content:
N = 5 replicas:  [ R1 ][ R2 ][ R3 ][ R4 ][ R5 ]

Write set (W=3):  R1   R2   R3
                  ===========
Read set  (R=3):            R3   R4   R5
                           ===========
                            ^
                            |
              overlap on R3  -> read sees the write

W + R = 6 > N = 5  ==> at least one shared replica

diagrams.title: CP vs AP Decision Under Partition
diagrams.content:
Partition detected
                        |
          +-------------+-------------+
          |                           |
    minority side                majority side
          |                           |
   +------+------+             +-------+------+
   |   CP path   |             |   CP path    |
   | reject write|             | accept write |
   | (no quorum) |             | (has quorum) |
   +-------------+             +--------------+

   +------+------+             +-------+------+
   |   AP path   |             |   AP path    |
   | accept write|             | accept write |
   | diverge     |             | diverge      |
   +-------------+             +--------------+
         \                          /
          +----- reconcile on heal -+

questions.question: State the CAP theorem precisely and explain why the 'pick two of three' framing is misleading.
questions.answer: CAP states that a distributed data store cannot simultaneously guarantee Consistency (linearizability), Availability (every request to a live node returns a non-error response), and Partition tolerance. The precise reading is conditional: when a network partition occurs, the system must choose between consistency and availability. The 'pick two' framing is misleading because partition tolerance is not a property you trade away, it is an environmental condition that real networks impose on you. Outside of partitions a well-built system is both consistent and available, so the trade-off only appears during the partition window. A clearer phrasing is 'if Partitioned, then Consistency or Availability.' Treating CA as a real system type usually just means partitions have not been considered.
followUps: What does the 'C' in CAP specifically mean?
Give an example of a CP and an AP system.

questions.question: Is CAP literally 'pick 2 of 3'? Defend your answer.
questions.answer: No. P is mandatory in any real distributed system because networks partition whether you plan for it or not, so you cannot meaningfully 'pick' to drop it. The actual choice is between C and A, and even that choice only applies during a partition. When the network is healthy, you get both C and A at once, so no two-of-three selection is happening in steady state. The triangle diagram also wrongly implies the three are symmetric resources; in reality P is a condition while C and A are responses to that condition. The honest model is the conditional 'PAC' plus the latency clause from PACELC. So 'pick two' is a teaching crutch that obscures the real engineering decision.
followUps: How would you rephrase CAP to avoid the confusion?
What does PACELC add?

questions.question: Can a single-node database be called 'CA'? Is that useful?
questions.answer: Technically yes: a single-node database has no inter-node network to partition, so it never faces the CAP trade-off and is trivially consistent and available. But the label is not useful, because the absence of partitions is a consequence of not being distributed, not a design achievement. The moment you replicate that database for durability or scale, partitions become possible and you must decide CP versus AP. So calling a single node 'CA' tells you nothing transferable about a distributed deployment. In interviews, the right move is to note that CA is the degenerate case and immediately ask what happens when the node is replicated. This reframes the conversation onto the real trade-off.
followUps: What changes when you add a synchronous replica?
What about an asynchronous replica?

questions.question: Explain the quorum condition W + R > N. Does satisfying it guarantee linearizability?
questions.answer: In a system of N replicas where writes wait for W acks and reads query R replicas, the condition W + R > N guarantees that the set of replicas written and the set read must overlap on at least one replica, so a read sees at least one copy of the most recent acknowledged write. This bounds staleness for that read. However, it does not guarantee linearizability. Concurrent writes can leave replicas with different values that the read must reconcile; read repair and anti-entropy run asynchronously; sloppy quorums with hinted handoff break the overlap assumption; and writes are not applied atomically across replicas, so a reader can catch a partial state. Quorums give probabilistic recency, not a single global order. Achieving true linearizability requires consensus or synchronous coordination on top of the quorum.
followUps: What is a sloppy quorum?
How would you add linearizability on top of a quorum store?

questions.question: What is PACELC and why is it considered a more complete model than CAP?
questions.answer: PACELC, introduced by Daniel Abadi, extends CAP with the common-case behavior: if there is a Partition (P), you trade between Availability and Consistency (A/C), Else (E, no partition) you trade between Latency and Consistency (L/C). It is more complete because partitions are rare, so CAP describes an edge case, whereas the Else branch describes the system's everyday life. Even on a perfectly healthy network, strong consistency requires coordination round-trips that add latency, and PACELC names that cost explicitly. For example, Spanner is PC/EC (consistency-first in both regimes, paying latency), while Cassandra is PA/EL (availability and low latency by default). Leading with PACELC in a design discussion signals that you reason about steady-state latency, not just dramatic failures.
followUps: Classify DynamoDB in PACELC terms.
Why does strong consistency cost latency even without a partition?

questions.question: Why is partition tolerance considered non-negotiable in real distributed systems?
questions.answer: Because partitions are caused by the physical and operational realities of networks, not by choices the designer makes. Links drop packets, switches and NICs fail, garbage-collection pauses freeze a node for seconds, firewalls get misconfigured, and cross-region latency spikes. Critically, a node cannot distinguish a crashed peer from a slow or unreachable one, so it must always be prepared for the possibility that messages are delayed or lost. If you design as though partitions cannot happen, you have not removed the failure mode, you have only removed your system's ability to respond to it correctly, leaving its behavior undefined when the partition inevitably occurs. Therefore P is assumed, and CAP reduces to the CP-versus-AP choice. This is also why the realistic spectrum of systems runs from CP to AP, never CA.
followUps: What is the difference between a crashed and a slow node from a peer's view?
How does this relate to FLP?

questions.question: Walk through Raft leader election and explain how it avoids two leaders in the same term.
questions.answer: Raft divides time into terms, each beginning with an election. A follower that receives no heartbeat within its randomized election timeout becomes a candidate, increments the current term, votes for itself, and sends RequestVote RPCs to peers. A node grants its vote at most once per term and only to a candidate whose log is at least as up to date as its own. A candidate becomes leader only upon receiving votes from a majority of the cluster. Because each node casts only one vote per term and a majority is required, two different candidates cannot both collect majorities in the same term, so there is at most one leader per term. Randomized timeouts make simultaneous candidacies (split votes) rare and self-correcting, since the next election uses fresh random timeouts. The up-to-date-log restriction additionally ensures a new leader already holds all committed entries.
followUps: How does Raft commit a log entry?
What is the purpose of the election restriction on log freshness?

questions.question: What is split-brain and why is a distributed lock alone insufficient to prevent data corruption?
questions.answer: Split-brain is when a partition or a faulty failover leaves two nodes each believing they are the leader, so both accept writes and the data diverges. A majority-quorum consensus system prevents two leaders from both committing, because two disjoint majorities cannot exist. However, a lock or lease alone is insufficient because a leader can acquire the lock, then pause (for example during a long GC), have its lease expire and a new leader take over, and then wake up and issue a write believing it still holds the lock. That stale write can corrupt the resource. The fix is fencing tokens: the lock service issues a monotonically increasing token with each grant, every write carries its token, and the resource rejects any token lower than the highest it has seen. This makes the deposed leader's late write harmless because it carries an older, smaller token.
followUps: Where should the fencing check be enforced?
How do quorums help with split-brain?

questions.question: Compare linearizability, sequential consistency, and causal consistency.
questions.answer: Linearizability is the strongest: all operations appear to take effect atomically at some point between their invocation and completion, consistent with real-time order, so a read always sees the latest committed write. Sequential consistency requires that all nodes observe operations in the same total order and that each process's operations appear in program order, but it does not require that order to respect real-time wall-clock ordering across processes. Causal consistency is weaker still: it only guarantees that operations that are causally related (one could have influenced the other) are seen in the same order by everyone, while concurrent operations may be observed in different orders on different replicas. The cost decreases as you weaken the model, and causal consistency is notable as the strongest model achievable while remaining available during a partition. Choosing the weakest model that preserves your invariants is the core skill.
followUps: Why is causal the strongest 'available' model?
How is read-your-writes related to causal consistency?

questions.question: How would you classify Cassandra and PostgreSQL in CAP terms, and what makes the answer subtle?
questions.answer: In its typical configuration PostgreSQL with synchronous replication is CP: it has a single leader, and a minority partition or a follower cannot accept writes without the leader, so it sacrifices availability to preserve a single consistent view. Cassandra is leaderless and AP by default: it keeps accepting reads and writes on reachable replicas during a partition and reconciles afterward. The subtlety is that both are tunable. Cassandra read and write at QUORUM give W + R > N overlap, producing CP-like behavior per operation, while a CL of ONE is firmly AP. PostgreSQL with an asynchronous replica can serve slightly stale reads from that replica, softening its strict CP posture. So the correct answer always specifies the configuration: 'in its default config X is ..., but it is tunable because ...'.
followUps: What Cassandra consistency level gives CP-like reads?
How does async replication weaken PostgreSQL's CP posture?

questions.question: Why does providing strong consistency cost latency even when there is no partition?
questions.answer: Strong consistency requires that a read observe the most recent committed write, which means writes and often reads must coordinate across replicas before returning. To commit a write a leader-based system must replicate to a majority and wait for their acknowledgements, and a linearizable read must either go through the leader or confirm leadership with a quorum round-trip to avoid returning stale data from a deposed leader. Each of these is a network round-trip, and across regions that round-trip can be tens or hundreds of milliseconds. Weaker models like eventual or read-your-writes can be served from the nearest replica without coordination, which is why they are faster. This latency cost is exactly the 'Else, Latency vs Consistency' branch of PACELC, and it is the reason strong consistency is something you spend deliberately rather than enable everywhere.
followUps: How can leader leases reduce read latency for linearizable reads?
When is a stale local read acceptable?

questions.question: When is last-write-wins (LWW) conflict resolution dangerous, and what are the alternatives?
questions.answer: LWW resolves concurrent writes by keeping the one with the highest timestamp and discarding the others. It is dangerous whenever silently dropping a write violates an invariant or loses real user intent, such as an inventory count, a shopping cart, or any monotonic accumulator. It is especially treacherous under clock skew, because a node with a fast clock can have its older write win over a genuinely newer one, and there is no record that data was lost. Safer alternatives include version vectors or vector clocks, which detect concurrency precisely so the application (or user) can merge; and CRDTs, which define a commutative, associative merge function so concurrent updates converge deterministically without coordination. CRDTs are ideal for counters, sets, and collaborative documents in AP systems. The rule of thumb: match the conflict strategy to the cost of losing an update, and reserve LWW for data where a lost write is harmless, like a cache entry.
followUps: How does a CRDT counter avoid losing increments?
What problem do vector clocks solve that timestamps do not?

questions.question: A leaderless store has N=5. Give a quorum config that tolerates two node failures while still guaranteeing read/write overlap, and explain the trade-offs.
questions.answer: With N=5, choosing W=3 and R=3 gives W + R = 6, which is greater than N=5, so the read and write sets always overlap on at least one replica. This config tolerates up to two replicas being down for writes (since N - W = 2) and likewise two down for reads. The trade-off is that every write must reach three replicas and every read must contact three, so both operations have higher tail latency than a W=1 or R=1 config, because they wait on the slowest of three responders. If you wanted cheaper writes you could pick W=2, R=4, which still overlaps (6 > 5) but makes reads slower and writes faster, useful for write-heavy workloads. Note that even with overlap you do not get linearizability, only bounded staleness, so for strict ordering you would need consensus. The choice is therefore a per-workload tuning of latency, durability, and failure tolerance.
followUps: What config maximizes write availability?
Why does overlap not give linearizability?

questions.question: Does a majority quorum in Raft mean the minority partition is completely useless?
questions.answer: Not necessarily, but it cannot make progress on writes. The minority side cannot form a majority, so it cannot elect a leader or commit new log entries, which is precisely how Raft preserves consistency during a partition (it gives up availability on that side). However, the minority can still serve some reads depending on the system's read semantics: if stale reads are acceptable, followers on the minority side can answer from their last known state, though those reads may be out of date. Strictly linearizable reads, by contrast, require contacting a quorum or confirming leadership, which the minority cannot do, so they will fail. Systems often expose this as a choice between linearizable reads (unavailable in the minority) and stale/bounded-staleness reads (available). So the minority is degraded, not entirely dead, and the exact behavior depends on the read consistency level configured.
followUps: How does etcd let you choose between linearizable and serializable reads?
What happens to the minority when the partition heals?

challenges.title: Design a Safe Quorum Configuration
challenges.prompt: You operate a leaderless, Dynamo-style key-value store with N = 5 replicas spread across availability zones. Product requires: (a) a read must never miss the latest acknowledged write (overlap guarantee), and (b) writes must still succeed when any 2 replicas are unavailable. Choose W and R, prove the requirements hold, then state precisely what your config does NOT guarantee and how you would close that gap if a feature needed linearizability.
hints: Overlap requires W + R > N. Failure tolerance for writes requires N - W >= number of tolerable failures.
Solve the two inequalities together for N = 5 and 2 tolerable write failures.
Remember that overlap bounds staleness but does not impose a global order; think about what would.
challenges.solutionCode:
Given:
  N = 5
  Requirement (a): W + R > N            // read/write overlap
  Requirement (b): N - W >= 2            // tolerate 2 write failures

Solve (b):  5 - W >= 2  ->  W <= 3
Choose the largest W that satisfies (b) for durability: W = 3

Solve (a):  W + R > N  ->  3 + R > 5  ->  R > 2  ->  R >= 3
Choose R = 3.

FINAL CONFIG:  N = 5, W = 3, R = 3   (W + R = 6 > 5)

Verification:
  Overlap:        3 + 3 = 6 > 5                 OK (>= 1 shared replica)
  Write failures: N - W = 5 - 3 = 2             OK (tolerates 2 down)
  Read failures:  N - R = 5 - 3 = 2             OK (tolerates 2 down)

What this does NOT guarantee:
  - NOT linearizable. Concurrent writes can leave divergent
    values that the reader must reconcile (e.g. via version
    vectors). Read repair / anti-entropy run asynchronously.
  - Sloppy quorums + hinted handoff would break overlap, because
    writes may land on non-home replicas. Disable sloppy quorum
    if requirement (a) must be strict.
  - A reader can observe a partially-applied write set (writes
    are not atomic across replicas).

Closing the gap to linearizability (if a feature needs it):
  Option 1: route that key's writes through a consensus group
            (Raft/Paxos) so a single ordered log defines truth.
  Option 2: use a coordinator with synchronous, ordered commit
            and leader leases for linearizable reads.
  Keep this CP path small and scoped to the keys that need it;
  leave the rest on the AP quorum config above.
challenges.explanation: The challenge forces the candidate to translate two product requirements into the two governing quorum inequalities and solve them jointly. Requirement (b), tolerating two write failures, bounds W from above via N - W >= 2, giving W <= 3; picking the largest allowed value W = 3 maximizes durability while still meeting the failure-tolerance budget. Requirement (a), the overlap guarantee, is the classic W + R > N condition, which with W = 3 and N = 5 forces R >= 3, so R = 3 is the minimal satisfying read width. The resulting config N=5, W=3, R=3 is then verified against both constraints and the symmetric read-failure tolerance. Crucially, the solution does not stop at the happy path: it explicitly states that overlap yields only bounded staleness, not linearizability, and enumerates the ways the guarantee can still be violated (concurrent divergent writes needing reconciliation, asynchronous read repair, sloppy quorums breaking overlap, and non-atomic multi-replica writes). Finally it shows the senior instinct of scoping a small CP path via consensus for the rare keys that genuinely need a global order, rather than forcing the whole store into expensive coordination. This demonstrates both the quorum math and the judgment about what the math does and does not buy you.

challenges.title: CP vs AP Counter Service
challenges.prompt: Design two variants of a globally-distributed 'likes' counter: a CP variant and an AP variant. For each, specify the write path, the read path, and the behavior during a network partition. Then enumerate the anomalies a client could observe in each variant and explain which workloads each variant is appropriate for. Express the design as structured pseudocode/config plus a written rationale.
hints: The CP variant should route increments through a consensus group; think about what the minority partition can and cannot do.
The AP variant should let every region accept increments locally; a CRDT counter (e.g. PN-Counter / G-Counter) avoids lost increments on merge.
List concrete anomalies: stale reads, temporary unavailability, non-monotonic counts, double counting on retries.
challenges.solutionCode:
=== CP VARIANT (consensus-backed) ===
config:
  store: Raft group, 5 nodes, majority = 3
  counter: single integer in the replicated log

write increment(key):
  leader.appendLog(INCR key)
  wait until majority persisted        // commit
  apply to state machine; return new value

read count(key):
  if linearizable:
     confirm leadership via quorum / leader lease
     return committed value
  else (serializable/stale):
     return local follower value (may lag)

during partition:
  majority side  -> elects/keeps leader, accepts increments
  minority side  -> NO leader, increments REJECTED / blocked
                    linearizable reads fail; stale reads optional

anomalies (CP):
  - temporary UNAVAILABILITY on minority during partition
  - higher write latency (cross-region majority round-trip)
  - double counting only if increments are not idempotent
    (fix: idempotency key per client request)


=== AP VARIANT (CRDT, available everywhere) ===
config:
  store: leaderless, one replica per region
  counter: PN-Counter  (per-replica increment vector)
           value = sum(P[r]) - sum(N[r]) over all replicas r

write increment(key):
  local_replica.P[self] += 1            // no coordination
  ack immediately
  async: gossip P/N vectors to peers

read count(key):
  return sum over known replicas of P[r] - N[r]   // may be low

merge(a, b):                            // commutative, idempotent
  for r: P[r] = max(a.P[r], b.P[r])
         N[r] = max(a.N[r], b.N[r])     // monotonic merge, no lost incr

during partition:
  every side accepts increments locally (AVAILABLE)
  reads return a possibly-stale, locally-summed value
  on heal: merge converges to the true total (no lost writes)

anomalies (AP):
  - STALE / UNDERCOUNTED reads until gossip converges
  - non-monotonic count across regions (region A may show 100,
    region B 97 momentarily) -> use session stickiness to hide
  - NEVER loses an increment thanks to per-replica max-merge


=== WHEN TO USE ===
CP: counts that gate a decision or must be exact at read time
    (quota limits, billing units, 'only 1 left in stock').
AP: vanity/social counters where availability and latency beat
    exactness, and eventual convergence is fine (likes, views).
challenges.explanation: This challenge tests whether the candidate can instantiate the abstract CP/AP trade-off in a concrete, common service and reason about the observable consequences of each choice. The CP variant routes every increment through a Raft majority, so the count is always a single agreed value and reads can be made linearizable, but the cost is explicit: the minority partition cannot accept increments and linearizable reads there fail, and every write pays a cross-region majority round-trip in latency. The candidate is expected to note that double counting is only a risk if increments are not idempotent, and to add a per-request idempotency key as the fix, since retries are common during recovery. The AP variant uses a PN-Counter CRDT so each region increments locally without coordination and the merge function takes a per-replica max, which is commutative, associative, and idempotent and therefore never loses an increment even when both sides of a partition accept writes concurrently. The key insight the explanation must surface is that the AP anomalies are bounded and benign (temporary undercounting and non-monotonic cross-region reads that converge on heal) and can be masked with session stickiness, whereas the CP anomaly is outright unavailability on the minority. Finally, mapping each variant to a workload, exact gating counts to CP and social vanity counts to AP, demonstrates the senior judgment of choosing the weakest sufficient guarantee rather than defaulting to strong consistency everywhere.

references.label: Brewer - CAP Twelve Years Later: How the Rules Have Changed
Gilbert & Lynch - Brewer's Conjecture and the Feasibility of CAP (proof)
Abadi - Consistency Tradeoffs in Modern Distributed Database Design (PACELC)
